summary: Kenneth documents a complex week of covered call and cash-secured put trades on HIMS, Nphase, D-Wave, and Archer Aviation. He demonstrates rolling cash-secured puts to manage assignment risk and accepts assignment on HIMS at $52 despite the stock trading at $46, betting on a near-term reversal based on technical analysis. The video illustrates real-world execution challenges of the wheel strategy when underlying stocks experience negative news cycles.
takeaways: Sell covered calls at strikes you'd be happy to be assigned at ($51 on HIMS, $35.50 on Nphase)
Rolling cash-secured puts costs money ($18.97 to roll HIMS from $57 to $52 strike) but can reduce losses when underlying drops sharply
Premium locked in on Wednesday ($469 on Nphase 3 contracts) may increase further—accept 'some of the money, not all of it' rather than chase maximum premium
Hold cash reserves equal to assignment amount ($15,000 for 3 HIMS contracts at $52 strike) to enable assignment execution
Accept assignment on underlying when fundamentally bullish despite unrealized losses, using technical support (50-day and 200-day moving averages) to time re-entry into covered calls
Use chart consolidation and news processing as entry signals for stepping into assigned positions at loss prices

strategy_candidates.change: Establish a pre-expiration rule: if underlying shows >15% gain from earnings low within 5 days of put expiration, roll down one strike or accept assignment rather than buying to close
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth accepted HIMS assignment at $52 when stock at $46 because he expected 'break out here' based on 50/200-day moving average consolidation and news digestion; formalized rule would prevent panic closing and reduce slippage

title: Part 11.5 8/15/25
